a: A container is a lightweight, isolated box that holds your app plus everything it needs to run, like libraries and settings. It runs the same way on any machine, so "it works on my laptop" also works in production.
fs: A container is an isolated process bundling an app with its dependencies, so it runs the same everywhere.
fj: Thinking a container is a full virtual machine. Containers share the host's kernel and are far lighter and faster than VMs.

a: An image is a read-only template, or snapshot, that contains your app and its dependencies. You build an image once, then start one or many running containers from it.
fs: An image is the immutable blueprint; a container is a running instance started from that image.
fj: Confusing image and container. The image is the recipe on disk; the container is the live thing running from it.

a: A Dockerfile is a small text file with step-by-step instructions for building an image. Each line says what base to start from, what files to copy, and what command to run.
fs: A Dockerfile is the declarative build script Docker reads to produce a repeatable image.
fj: Editing a running container by hand instead of the Dockerfile. Changes made inside a container vanish; the Dockerfile is the source of truth.

q: What does 'docker build' and 'docker run' do?
a: 'docker build' reads your Dockerfile and produces an image. 'docker run' takes an image and starts it as a live container you can use.
fs: Build turns a Dockerfile into an image; run turns an image into a running container.
fj: Expecting 'docker run' to pick up your latest code edits. You must rebuild the image first, or the container uses the old snapshot.

a: A volume is storage that lives outside the container, so your data survives even when the container is deleted or rebuilt. Use it for databases, uploads, or anything you want to keep.
fs: A volume persists data outside the container's writable layer so it outlives the container lifecycle.
fj: Assuming files written inside a container stick around. Without a volume, that data is lost the moment the container is removed.
